content:
# Genesis Chapter 32 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 18v–19r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_032_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_032_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 29–30 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0033

---

**1** Now Jacob went on his way, and the angels of God met him.

**2** And when Jacob saw them, he said, "This is the camp of God." So he called the name of that place Mahanaim.

**3** And Jacob sent messengers before him to Esau his brother in the land of Seir, the country of Edom,

**4** instructing them, "Thus you shall say to my lord Esau: Thus says your servant Jacob, 'I have sojourned with Laban and stayed until now.

**5** I have oxen and donkeys, flocks, male servants and female servants. I have sent to tell my lord, in order that I may find favor in your eyes.'"

**6** And the messengers returned to Jacob, saying, "We came to your brother Esau, and he is coming to meet you, and four hundred men are with him."

**7** Then Jacob was greatly afraid and distressed. He divided the people who were with him, and the flocks and herds and camels, into two camps,

**8** thinking, "If Esau comes to the one camp and strikes it, then the camp that is left will escape."

**9** And Jacob said, "O God of my father Abraham and God of my father Isaac, O LORD who said to me, 'Return to your country and to your kindred, and I will do you good,'

**10** I am not worthy of the least of all the steadfast love and all the faithfulness that you have shown to your servant, for with only my staff I crossed this Jordan, and now I have become two camps.

**11** Please deliver me from the hand of my brother, from the hand of Esau, for I fear him, lest he come and strike me, the mothers with the children.

**12** But you said, 'I will surely do you good, and make your offspring as the sand of the sea, which cannot be numbered for multitude.'"

**13** So he lodged there that night, and from what he had with him he took a present for his brother Esau:

**14** two hundred female goats and twenty male goats, two hundred ewes and twenty rams,

**15** thirty milking camels and their young, forty cows and ten bulls, twenty female donkeys and ten male donkeys.

**16** These he handed over to his servants, every drove by itself, and said to his servants, "Pass on before me and put a space between drove and drove."

**17** He instructed the first, "When Esau my brother meets you and asks you, 'To whom do you belong? Where are you going? And whose are these ahead of you?'

**18** then you shall say, 'They belong to your servant Jacob. It is a present sent to my lord Esau. And moreover, behold, he is behind us.'"

**19** He likewise instructed the second and the third and all who followed the droves, "You shall say the same thing to Esau when you find him,

**20** and you shall say, 'Moreover, your servant Jacob is behind us.'" For he thought, "I may appease him with the present that goes before me, and afterward I will see his face. Perhaps he will accept me."

**21** So the present passed on before him, and he himself lodged that night in the camp.

**22** The same night he arose and took his two wives, his two female servants, and his eleven children, and crossed the ford of the Jabbok.

**23** He took them and sent them across the stream, and he sent across everything that he had.

**24** And Jacob was left alone. And a man wrestled with him until the breaking of the day.

**25** When the man saw that he did not prevail against Jacob, he touched the socket of his hip, and Jacob's hip was put out of joint as he wrestled with him.

**26** Then he said, "Let me go, for the day has broken." But Jacob said, "I will not let you go unless you bless me."

**27** And he said to him, "What is your name?" And he said, "Jacob."

**28** Then he said, "Your name shall no longer be called Jacob, but Israel, for you have striven with God and with men, and have prevailed."

**29** Then Jacob asked him, "Please tell me your name." But he said, "Why is it that you ask my name?" And he blessed him there.

**30** So Jacob called the name of the place Peniel, saying, "For I have seen God face to face, and yet my life has been delivered."

**31** The sun rose upon him as he passed Penuel, limping because of his hip.

**32** Therefore the people of Israel do not eat the sinew of the thigh that is on the socket of the hip to this day, because he touched the socket of Jacob's hip on the sinew of the thigh.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 32 spans Folios 18v–19r across two pages.

   **Folio 18v** (GEN_032_page_001.jpg): The verso of Folio 18, presented in inverted orientation per WSRP imaging convention. This page carries the end of Genesis 31 (the Laban farewell) and then the full opening of Genesis 32. The chapter-division is visible after Laban's departure. Genesis 32 begins with Jacob's departure from the covenant site and his encounter with the angels at Mahanaim (v. 1–2), the dispatch of messengers to Esau (vv. 3–6), Jacob's division of his camp and his prayer (vv. 7–12), and the preparation of the elaborate gift-caravan (vv. 13–21). The three-column layout with Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks is clearly legible throughout. Masorah parva notes are visible in the inter-column margins.

   **Folio 19r** (GEN_032_page_002.jpg): The recto of Folio 19, with the folio number visible in the upper margin. This page carries Genesis 32:22 through 32:33 (the MT verse count through the Jabbok wrestling, the naming of Israel, the naming of Peniel, and the dietary prohibition), and continues into the opening of Genesis 33. The wrestling narrative, the renaming of Jacob as Israel, the naming of Penuel, and the dietary prohibition at the chapter's close are all clearly visible. The text is legible throughout with characteristic Masoretic pointing and te'amim.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two pages were read directly.

   **Page 29** (GEN_032_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 29 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0033). The chapter marker "CAP. XXXVI" is visible at the top center of the page in the header area. This page continues the end of Genesis 31 and carries the LXX material corresponding to Genesis 32:1 through approximately 32:21. The continuous uncial Greek text in three columns is legible. The LXX numbering is one verse behind the MT through this chapter (see versification note below).

   **Page 30** (GEN_032_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 30 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0034). The chapter marker "CAP. XXXVII" is visible at the top center. This page carries the Jabbok wrestling narrative and its aftermath, corresponding to Genesis 32:22–33 MT. The wrestling scene, the renaming to Israel, the naming of Peniel/Penuel, and the dietary notice are all present.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. Versification — MT vs. LXX offset**

The Masoretic Text numbers Genesis 32 with 33 verses. The LXX (and some English traditions) number differently because what the MT counts as Genesis 31:55 ("Laban rose early in the morning…") is numbered as Genesis 32:1 in the LXX versification, shifting every subsequent verse in chapter 32 forward by one number. The formal translation follows the standard MT versification (33 verses), as used in most formal English translations (ESV, NASB, NKJV, NIV). The LXX text of Vaticanus is cited by its Vaticanus/LXX content rather than its verse number when the two systems diverge.

**2. "Mahanaim" — מַחֲנַיִם (v. 2)**

The place name מַחֲנַיִם (Machanayim) is the dual form of מַחֲנֶה (machaneh — "camp"), meaning "two camps." The naming is explicit and immediate: Jacob sees the angels, declares "This is the camp of God," and names the place. The dual form implicitly foreshadows Jacob's own division of his company into two camps in verse 7–8 — the narrative is threading a pattern of doubling (mahanaim / two camps / two droves / the two brothers). The formal translation retains "Mahanaim" as a proper name without translation, following the convention of all formal English translations. The LXX renders Μαναιμ (Manaim), a straightforward transliteration.

**3. "The camp of God" — מַחֲנֵה אֱלֹהִים (v. 2)**

Jacob's exclamation uses מַחֲנֵה (machaneh — singular "camp") before giving the place the dual name Mahanaim. The singular "camp of God" contrasts with the immediately following dual — Jacob first perceives a single divine camp, then names the place "two camps." Some translators resolve this by rendering "the camps of God" (plural), matching the dual place name. The formal translation renders "the camp of God" (singular) following the Hebrew's singular form in the exclamation. The structural movement from singular to dual is part of the text's subtle artistry.

**4. "My lord Esau" and "your servant Jacob" — אֲדֹנִי עֵשָׂו / עַבְדְּךָ יַעֲקֹב (vv. 4–5, 18–20)**

Jacob's instructions to his messengers carefully deploy the vocabulary of social hierarchy: he repeatedly refers to himself as "your servant" (עַבְדְּךָ, avdekha) and to Esau as "my lord" (אֲדֹנִי, adoni). This is more than politeness; it is a deliberate reversal of the blessing Jacob stole from Isaac, which declared that Esau would "serve" Jacob (27:40). Here Jacob performs submission to the brother he cheated. The formal translation preserves "my lord" and "your servant" throughout (vv. 4, 5, 18, 20) — consistent with the Hebrew and essential to the theological irony of the scene. The LXX renders κύριος (kyrios — "lord") and παῖς / δοῦλος ("servant/slave"), consistent with the MT.

**5. "I am not worthy of the least" — קָטֹנְתִּי מִכֹּל הַחֲסָדִים (v. 10)**

The Hebrew verb קָטֹן (qaton — "to be small, to be insignificant") in the perfect first person (קָטֹנְתִּי, qatonti — "I have become too small / I am too small") combined with מִכֹּל (mikkol — "from all / for all") yields a clause of remarkable humility: "I am too small for all the steadfast love." The formal translation renders "I am not worthy of the least of all the steadfast love and all the faithfulness" — preserving the sense of comprehensive unworthiness while allowing natural English. The rendering "not worthy of the least" honors the superlative force of qatonti mikkol. The LXX renders ἱκανοῦμαι ἐν πάσῃ δικαιοσύνῃ (hikanoumai en pasē dikaiosynē — "I am sufficient in all righteousness"), a striking departure from the MT's humble self-abasement; the LXX appears to have read the phrase differently or follows a different Vorlage. The formal translation follows the MT, which is the more internally coherent reading given the penitential tone of the prayer.

**6. "Steadfast love" for חֶסֶד (v. 10)**

The key theological term חֶסֶד (chesed) is rendered "steadfast love" throughout this translation, consistent with the rendering established in Genesis 24 and following. The term encompasses loyalty, covenant faithfulness, love, and kindness. "Steadfast love" is the most widely used formal equivalent rendering (ESV, NRSV) and best captures the relational-covenantal dimension of the Hebrew. "Lovingkindness" (NASB, KJV) is a traditional alternative but has a more archaic register. "Faithfulness" (וֶאֱמֶת, ve-emet — "and faithfulness/truth") is the paired term in verse 10 and is rendered straightforwardly.

**7. "With only my staff I crossed this Jordan" — כִּי בְמַקְלִי עָבַרְתִּי אֶת הַיַּרְדֵּן הַזֶּה (v. 10)**

Jacob's prayer recalls his first crossing of the Jordan when he left Canaan — alone, with nothing but his staff. The phrase "with only my staff" (bemaqli — "with my staff alone") captures the contrast with his current condition: two wives, two servants, eleven children, vast flocks and herds. The Jordan is explicitly named, providing a geographical and narrative frame: Jacob left across the Jordan with nothing and returns across the Jordan as two camps. The formal translation renders "with only my staff I crossed this Jordan" — preserving the personal pronoun "this" (hazzeh, indicating the specific Jordan he is now preparing to cross again).

**8. "A man wrestled with him" — וַיֵּאָבֵק אִישׁ עִמּוֹ (v. 24)**

The identity of the "man" (אִישׁ, ish) who wrestles with Jacob is deliberately withheld. The text says only "a man" — the ambiguity is fundamental to the narrative's power. Jacob himself says he has "seen God face to face" (v. 30). The "man" demands that Jacob let him go before daylight, suggesting a being whose power is limited by the dawn. The "man" knows Jacob's name and renames him. Yet he refuses to reveal his own name. The formal translation renders "a man" exactly as the Hebrew — neither "an angel" nor "God" — preserving the text's deliberate opacity. The LXX renders ἄνθρωπος (anthrōpos — "a man/person"), consistent with the MT.

**9. The Jabbok — יַבֹּק — and the triple wordplay (v. 22–28)**

The Hebrew text embeds a remarkable threefold wordplay that is intrinsic to the theology of the passage:
- יַעֲקֹב (Ya'aqov — "Jacob") — the patriarch's name, rooted in עָקֵב (aqev — "heel")
- יַבֹּק (Yabboq — "Jabbok") — the river name
- וַיֵּאָבֵק (vaye'aveq — "and he wrestled") — the verb used for wrestling

The sounds yod-ayin-qoph / yod-bet-qoph / yod-aleph-bet-qoph interweave across the passage. The place (Jabbok) and the action (wrestling) both echo the person (Jacob). At the Jabbok, Jacob's name-identity is both enacted and abolished: he wrestles (ye'aveq) at the Yabboq and emerges no longer Ya'aqov but Yisra'el. This wordplay cannot be reproduced in English. The formal translation preserves all three forms — "Jacob," "Jabbok," "wrestled" — and notes the wordplay here rather than attempting an artificial English equivalent. The LXX renders Ιαββοκ (Iabbok) for the river and πάλαιω (palaiō — "to wrestle/struggle") for the action, losing the Hebrew wordplay entirely.

**10. "The socket of his hip" — כַּף יְרֵכוֹ (v. 25)**

The Hebrew כַּף (kaf) normally means "palm" or "hollow" — the curved cup-shape of the hand. When used with יָרֵךְ (yarekh — "thigh/hip"), it designates the hip socket, the cup-shaped hollow of the joint. The formal translation renders "the socket of his hip" — a precise anatomical rendering that captures both the shape implied by kaf and the medical reality of what occurs (hip dislocation). The LXX renders τὸ πλάτος τοῦ μηροῦ αὐτοῦ (to platos tou mērou autou — "the breadth of his thigh"), a different anatomical understanding. The formal translation follows the MT rendering of the socket/cup.

**11. "For you have striven with God and with men" — כִּי שָׂרִיתָ עִם אֱלֹהִים וְעִם אֲנָשִׁים (v. 28)**

The renaming of Jacob to Israel (יִשְׂרָאֵל, Yisra'el) is explained with the verb שָׂרָה (sarah — "to strive, to contend, to struggle"). The name Israel is thus understood as "one who strives with God" or "God strives" (from sarah + El). The etymological explanation — "for you have striven (sarita) with God (Elohim) and with men (anashim), and have prevailed (vatukhal)" — is given directly by the text. The formal translation preserves "striven" as the English verb, which most closely captures sarah's meaning in context. The LXX renders ἐνίσχυσας μετὰ θεοῦ καὶ μετὰ ἀνθρώπων δυνατός (enischysas meta theou kai meta anthrōpōn dynatos — "you were strong with God and with men, powerful"), substituting strength/power for the striving/prevailing dynamic of the MT.

**12. "Why is it that you ask my name?" — לָמָּה זֶּה תִּשְׁאַל לִשְׁמִי (v. 29)**

The mysterious being's refusal to reveal his name is expressed with a question that carries both rebuke and wonder. The demonstrative זֶּה (zeh — "this") adds a nuance: "Why is it — this thing — that you ask for my name?" The withholding of the name is theologically significant: in the ancient Near East, knowing the divine name granted power over the deity; this being will give Jacob a name but will not surrender his own. The formal translation renders "Why is it that you ask my name?" — preserving the interrogative form and the slight emphasis of the demonstrative. The LXX renders διὰ τί τοῦτο ἐρωτᾷς τὸ ὄνομά μου (dia ti touto erōtas to onoma mou — "why is this that you ask my name?"), consistent with the MT.

**13. "Peniel" / "Penuel" — פְּנִיאֵל / פְּנוּאֵל (vv. 30–31)**

Two slightly different forms of the place name appear in adjacent verses: פְּנִיאֵל (Peni'el) in verse 30 (Jacob's naming declaration) and פְּנוּאֵל (Penu'el) in verse 31 (the narrator's passing reference as Jacob walks away). Both forms mean "face of God" (pene + El). The variation between the two spellings is textually attested in the Leningrad Codex and reflected in the LXX (Φανουηλ in v. 31). The formal translation preserves both spellings as they appear: "Peniel" in verse 30 (the theological moment of naming) and "Penuel" in verse 31 (the geographical reference). This follows the MT precisely.

**14. "My life has been delivered" — וַתִּנָּצֵל נַפְשִׁי (v. 30)**

The verb נָצַל (natsal — "to be delivered, rescued, snatched away") in the Niphal (passive) form — "my life was delivered / my soul escaped" — expresses the astonishment of survival. Jacob expected death from seeing God face to face; instead he survived. The noun נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh — "soul, life, being") is rendered "my life" throughout the formal translation. The full clause "I have seen God face to face, and yet my life has been delivered" captures the contrast between the peril and the survival. The LXX renders γὰρ εἶδον θεὸν πρόσωπον πρὸς πρόσωπον καὶ ἐσώθη μου ἡ ψυχή (gar eidon theon prosōpon pros prosōpon kai esōthē mou hē psychē — "for I have seen God face to face and my soul was saved"), consistent with the MT.

**15. "The sinew of the thigh" — גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה (v. 32)**

The phrase גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה (gid ha-nasheh) designates a specific anatomical structure — the sciatic nerve or the tendon of the hip joint. The word גִּיד (gid) means "sinew, tendon, nerve." The word הַנָּשֶׁה (ha-nasheh) is more difficult: it may derive from נָשָׁה (nashah — "to forget, to displace") — hence "the dislocating sinew" or "the sinew of displacement" — or it may be an anatomical technical term. The formal translation renders "the sinew of the thigh" (following the anatomical rendering of KJV/ESV/NASB), which is standard in formal English translations. "The socket of the hip" (kaf ha-yarekh) in the same verse refers to the hip socket (see note 10). The two terms appear to refer to distinct anatomical structures: the socket/joint on the one hand and the sinew/tendon on the other. The LXX renders τὸ νεῦρον (to neuron — "the nerve/sinew"), consistent with the MT's gid.

**16. Verse 32 — the dietary prohibition as etiological conclusion**

The chapter ends with an etiological note explaining a dietary custom observed by "the people of Israel" to the narrator's "this day": they do not eat the sinew of the thigh on the hip socket, because this is where Jacob's wrestling wound occurred. The phrase "the people of Israel" (בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, benei Yisra'el — "the sons/children of Israel") appears here for the first time in Genesis — the first time Jacob's new name Israel is used as a collective tribal designation. The formal translation renders "the people of Israel" (following ESV/NIV) rather than "the children of Israel" (KJV/NASB), as "people" better captures the national-corporate sense in contemporary English. The LXX renders οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ (hoi huioi Israēl — "the sons of Israel"), closer to the literal Hebrew.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 32

Pages 29–30 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text across both pages was legible. Key variants observed:

| Verse (MT) | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 32:2 | מַחֲנֵה אֱלֹהִים ("camp of God," singular) | παρεμβολὴ θεοῦ ("camp of God," singular) | "camp of God" | LXX consistent with MT; singular preserved |
| 32:10 | קָטֹנְתִּי מִכֹּל הַחֲסָדִים ("I am too small for all the steadfast love") | ἱκανοῦμαι ἐν πάσῃ δικαιοσύνῃ ("I am sufficient in all righteousness") | "I am not worthy of the least of all the steadfast love" | LXX appears to follow a different Vorlage or different reading; formal translation follows MT |
| 32:24 | אִישׁ ("a man") | ἄνθρωπος ("a man/person") | "a man" | MT and LXX agree; formal translation preserves the deliberate ambiguity |
| 32:28 | שָׂרִיתָ עִם אֱלֹהִים ("you have striven with God") | ἐνίσχυσας μετὰ θεοῦ ("you were strong with God") | "you have striven with God" | LXX reads strength/power; MT reads striving/struggling; formal translation follows MT's verb |
| 32:29 | לָמָּה זֶּה תִּשְׁאַל לִשְׁמִי ("why is it that you ask my name?") | διὰ τί τοῦτο ἐρωτᾷς τὸ ὄνομά μου ("why is this that you ask my name?") | "Why is it that you ask my name?" | MT and LXX consistent |
| 32:30 | פְּנִיאֵל Peni'el | Φανουηλ (Phanouel) | "Peniel" | LXX spelling reflects a slightly different vocalization tradition |
| 32:31 | פְּנוּאֵל Penu'el | Φανουηλ (Phanouel) | "Penuel" | LXX uses a single form for both verses; formal translation preserves the MT's two distinct spellings |
| 32:32 | בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל ("the sons/children of Israel") | οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ("the sons of Israel") | "the people of Israel" | LXX follows MT literally; formal translation renders corporate-national sense with "people" |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Structure of Genesis 32: Three Encounters**

Genesis 32 is organized around three encounters that progressively intensify Jacob's vulnerability and transformation. The first encounter is with the angels at Mahanaim (vv. 1–2): a brief, benign encounter that frames Jacob's re-entry into the land as divinely witnessed. The second encounter is anticipated but not yet realized — the news that Esau is coming with four hundred men (vv. 3–21): this encounter generates the chapter's fear, prayer, and elaborate gift-giving. The third encounter is the one that actually occurs — the nocturnal wrestling at the Jabbok (vv. 22–32): an encounter with a being whose identity is withheld, which leaves Jacob permanently marked and permanently renamed. The structure moves from the heavenly (angels) through the earthly-feared (Esau) to the mysterious (the man).

**The Prayer of Jacob (vv. 9–12): The First Extended Petition in the Patriarchal Narratives**

Jacob's prayer in verses 9–12 is the most formally structured prayer in the Genesis patriarchal narrative — longer and more theologically articulate than any prayer of Abraham or Isaac. It opens with a threefold divine address (God of my father Abraham / God of my father Isaac / O LORD who said to me), moves to a promise recollection (v. 9b), then to a confession of unworthiness (v. 10), then to a specific petition (v. 11), and closes by citing the divine promise as the ground of the request (v. 12). This structure anticipates the developed prayer traditions of the Hebrew Bible (Psalms of lament, Nehemiah 1, Daniel 9). Jacob's prayer is also the first in Genesis to use the divine name YHWH (the LORD) directly in address, suggesting a new register of covenant intimacy.

**The Gift-Caravan as Atonement (vv. 13–21): כִּי אֲכַפְּרָה פָנָיו**

The word Jacob uses for "appease" in verse 20 — אֲכַפְּרָה (akhappera) — is the piel cohortative of כָּפַר (kaphar), the verb whose noun form כַּפֹּרֶת (kaporet) gives us the "mercy seat" and whose Aramaic cognate underlies Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement). Jacob is not merely sending a diplomatic gift; the text's word choice suggests a quasi-atoning action — he is attempting to "cover" (kaphar) the face of his brother, to make expiation for the wrong he has done. The formal translation renders this "appease" (v. 20) following the standard formal equivalent tradition, with this note flagging the atonement vocabulary.

**Jacob Left Alone — וַיִּוָּתֵר יַעֲקֹב לְבַדּוֹ (v. 24)**

The verse "Jacob was left alone" (vayyivvater Ya'aqov levaddo) is one of the most loaded sentences in Genesis. The verb יָתַר (yatar — "to be left over, to remain, to be left behind") carries the sense of a remainder — Jacob is what is left when everything else has been sent across. His isolation is complete. It is in this aloneness that the wrestling begins. The formal translation renders "Jacob was left alone" — the simplest possible English that preserves the emphasis on solitude. The narrative's theological logic is that Jacob's most transforming encounter occurs when he has sent away everything and everyone, and stands with nothing between himself and the darkness.

**The Name Withheld — "Why is it that you ask my name?" (v. 29)**

The mysterious wrestler's refusal to give his name stands in deliberate contrast to the divine self-disclosure at the burning bush (Exodus 3:14 — "I AM WHO I AM") and at Sinai. Here, the being who knows Jacob's name and changes it will not surrender its own. The asymmetry of naming — Jacob named, the other un-named — reinforces the inscrutability of the encounter. The formal translation renders the question precisely as asked, making no explanatory addition. The text's restraint is itself the point.

**Peniel / Penuel — The Face of God Survived (vv. 30–31)**

Jacob's exclamation "I have seen God face to face, and yet my life has been delivered" employs the Hebrew idiom פָּנִים אֶל פָּנִים (panim el panim — "face to face") that recurs at key moments in the relationship between God and humanity (Exodus 33:11 — Moses spoke with God "face to face"; Deuteronomy 34:10 — "there has not arisen a prophet in Israel like Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face"). The place name Peniel / Penuel (face of God) memorializes the encounter permanently in the landscape. The sun rising on Jacob as he passes Penuel (v. 31) creates a visual image — the limping patriarch in the morning light — that is one of the most vivid scene-closings in all of Genesis.

**The Dietary Prohibition (v. 32): Narrative Law**

Verse 32 is unusual in the narrative books of the Hebrew Bible: it is an etiological statement in the form of a law, embedded in a narrative chapter with no formal legal introduction. The prohibition against eating the sinew of the thigh functions as a living commemoration — every time Israel does not eat this part of the animal, Jacob's wound at the Jabbok is remembered in the body of the community. The text thus anticipates the commemorative logic of later Israelite law (Passover as commemoration of the Exodus, Sabbath as commemoration of creation). The appearance of "the children/people of Israel" (benei Yisra'el) here for the first time as a collective designation marks the moment when the individual's wound becomes the community's practice.
